_meta.null_signal_reason: No new FIMI operations, platform disclosures, or government attributions dated May 9-15, 2026 were found across all Tier 1-3 sources. Most recent Tier 1 material is Meta H1 2026 report (March 11, 2026) and Google TAG Q4 2025 bulletin (January 29, 2026). Weekly research bundle confirms quiet-week status.
_meta.cycle_disposition_reason: Weekly research bundle returned no new-this-week material; all campaigns are carry-forward references from prior cycles; daily collector found zero findings; reasoner analysis confirmed no attribution changes.

lead_signal.headline: No new FIMI operations identified this week — structural reporting gap across all Tier 1-3 sources
lead_signal.body: Exhaustive search across Meta CIB, Google TAG, Microsoft MSTIC, EEAS EUvsDisinfo, Stanford IO, DFRLab, ASPI, and Bellingcat returned no new FIMI-specific disclosures, CIB reports, government attributions, or institutional publications dated May 9-15, 2026. Most recent actionable Tier 1 material remains Meta H1 2026 Adversarial Threat Report (March 11, 2026) and Google TAG Q4 2025 Bulletin (January 29, 2026). This represents a structural reporting gap rather than operational pause. Russia, China, and Iran remain assessed as highly active or active based on prior baseline disclosures, but no new evidence emerged this cycle.

module_0.title: No new FIMI operations identified this week — structural reporting gap across all Tier 1-3 sources
module_0.body: Exhaustive search across Meta CIB, Google TAG, Microsoft MSTIC, EEAS EUvsDisinfo, Stanford IO, DFRLab, ASPI, and Bellingcat returned no new FIMI-specific disclosures, CIB reports, government attributions, or institutional publications dated May 9-15, 2026. Most recent actionable Tier 1 material remains Meta H1 2026 Adversarial Threat Report (March 11, 2026) and Google TAG Q4 2025 Bulletin (January 29, 2026). This represents a structural reporting gap rather than operational pause. Russia, China, and Iran remain assessed as highly active or active based on prior baseline disclosures, but no new evidence emerged this cycle.

actors.posture: Persistent multi-platform CIB operations targeting post-Soviet periphery (Moldova, Armenia), Western Europe, and Ukraine narratives. Increasing use of unwitting freelance social media managers in Africa as operational cutouts. Portal Kombat domain network remains active across Google surfaces.
actors.doctrine_note: No new doctrine developments this week. Baseline posture unchanged from Meta H1 2026 and Google TAG Q4 2025 disclosures.
actors.headline: Russia remains the most documented FIMI actor across Tier 1 sources; no new disruption disclosed this week.
actors.asymmetric: Absence of new disclosure does not equal absence of activity. Russia assessed as operationally active despite reporting gap.

actors.posture: High-volume spam-and-pivot model (Spamouflage/Taizi Flood): mass account acquisition, spammy lifestyle content as cover, with a small subset pivoting to political narratives targeting US-China relations, Tibet, and Taiwan. Increasing sophistication noted in Meta H1 2026 report.
actors.doctrine_note: No new doctrine developments this week. Baseline posture unchanged from Meta H1 2026 disclosure.
actors.headline: China assessed as one of three leading global FIMI sources; no new platform disclosure this week.
actors.asymmetric: High-volume operations continue but are underdocumented in weekly disclosure cycles. Structural gap in real-time visibility.

actors.posture: Iran continues cyber-enabled influence operations via IUVM and Cotton Sandstorm. Endless Mayfly attribution updated to IUVM in Meta H1 2026. New English-language operation targeting Western audiences disrupted in Q4 2025 before audience-building phase. IRGC EU terrorist designation (2023) has not visibly degraded operational tempo.
actors.doctrine_note: No new doctrine developments this week. IUVM attribution baseline established in Meta H1 2026.
actors.headline: Iran IUVM attribution confirmed in Meta H1 2026; no new disruption this week.
actors.asymmetric: Structural OSINT gap on IRGC-linked operations outside Meta and Google visibility. IRGC EU terrorist designation has not produced measurable operational degradation.

actors.posture: Structural OSINT gap: Gulf-state FIMI operations are underrepresented in Tier 1-3 disclosures due to commercial relationships between Gulf states and Western platform infrastructure, and limited civil-society monitoring capacity in the region. No new disclosure found this week.
actors.doctrine_note: No new doctrine developments this week. Structural coverage gap persists.
actors.headline: No new Gulf-state FIMI disclosure found this week; structural coverage gap persists.
actors.asymmetric: Absence of disclosure does not equal absence of activity. Lower confidence is structurally correct, not an analytical failure.

actors.posture: US-origin influence operations are structurally underreported in Tier 1-3 sources due to domestic legal constraints on attribution, platform reluctance to designate US-origin CIB, and the GEC reduced public output posture in 2025-2026. No new disclosure found this week.
actors.doctrine_note: No new doctrine developments this week. Structural coverage gap persists.
actors.headline: No new US-origin FIMI disclosure found this week; structural coverage gap persists.
actors.asymmetric: EEAS FIMI Explorer does not record this actor by documented political design rather than evidentiary threshold. Absence of disclosure reflects institutional perimeter, not operational absence.

actors.posture: Israeli-origin influence operations are structurally underreported in Tier 1-3 sources due to platform reluctance to designate Israeli-origin CIB and EEAS institutional perimeter exclusion by documented political design. No new disclosure found this week.
actors.doctrine_note: No new doctrine developments this week. Structural coverage gap persists.
actors.headline: No new Israeli-origin FIMI disclosure found this week; structural coverage gap persists.
actors.asymmetric: EEAS FIMI Explorer does not record this actor by documented political design rather than evidentiary threshold. EP PEGA Committee (2022-2023) documented specific operations meeting DISARM TTP criteria that were not entered into the FIMI Explorer.

disclosure_gaps.gap_description: No CIB-equivalent disclosure mechanism. Structural gap persists with no weekly transparency reporting.
No new CIB report published since H1 2026 Adversarial Threat Report (March 11, 2026). Next expected disclosure: H2 2026 report (estimated September 2026).
No new TAG bulletin published since Q4 2025 (January 29, 2026). Next expected disclosure: Q1 2026 bulletin (estimated April-May 2026, now overdue).
No new MSTIC or MTAC influence-operations post published in May 9-15 window.

vectors.name: Platform Opacity
vectors.headline: No new Tier 1 platform disclosures this week; Meta and Google TAG reporting cycles create multi-week visibility gaps
vectors.summary: Meta H1 2026 report (March 11) and Google TAG Q4 2025 bulletin (January 29) remain the most recent Tier 1 disclosures. No new CIB reports, TAG bulletins, or MSTIC posts were published in the May 9-15 window. X/Twitter structural gap persists with no CIB-equivalent mechanism.
vectors.detail: Platform transparency is structurally episodic rather than continuous. Meta publishes biannual Adversarial Threat Reports; Google TAG publishes quarterly bulletins; Microsoft MSTIC publishes ad hoc. This creates multi-week gaps where no new Tier 1 attribution evidence is available. The current gap (9 weeks since Meta, 15 weeks since Google TAG) is within normal reporting cycles but leaves real-time FIMI monitoring dependent on Tier 2-3 sources with lower attribution confidence. X/Twitter has no CIB-equivalent disclosure mechanism, creating a permanent structural gap for one of the largest FIMI distribution platforms.
vectors.asymmetric: Reporting cadence gaps are a structural feature of platform transparency, not an incidental failure. Real-time FIMI monitoring is impossible under current disclosure models.

vectors.name: Attribution Gap
vectors.headline: Gulf, US, and Israeli FIMI operations remain structurally underdocumented in Tier 1-3 sources
vectors.summary: Russia, China, and Iran dominate Tier 1 platform disclosures. Gulf-state, US-origin, and Israeli-origin FIMI operations are structurally underrepresented due to commercial relationships, domestic legal constraints, and EEAS institutional perimeter exclusion by documented political design.
vectors.detail: Six-actor parity is the methodological standard, but evidentiary parity is not achievable under current institutional and platform disclosure models. Russia and China are tracked by EEAS, Meta, Google TAG, and MSTIC. Iran is tracked by Meta and MSTIC. Gulf states, US, and Israel are not systematically tracked by any Tier 1 source. This is not an analytical failure but a structural feature of the FIMI monitoring ecosystem. Lower confidence for Gulf, US, and Israeli operations is structurally correct.
vectors.asymmetric: Attribution gap is not evenly distributed. It systematically favors actors with adversarial relationships to Western platforms and institutions, creating a structural bias in FIMI visibility.

vectors.name: Narrative Persistence
vectors.headline: No new narrative-persistence data this week; baseline campaigns (Doppelganger, Endless Mayfly, Portal Kombat) assessed as ongoing
vectors.summary: Doppelganger (RU), Endless Mayfly (IR), and Portal Kombat (RU) remain assessed as ongoing based on prior Tier 1 disclosures. No new evidence of narrative persistence emerged this week.
vectors.detail: Narrative persistence is measured by coordinated messaging across 3+ weeks. Doppelganger, Endless Mayfly, and Portal Kombat meet this threshold based on Meta H1 2026 and Google TAG Q4 2025 disclosures. No new evidence this week, but absence of disclosure does not equal absence of activity. Baseline assessment: these campaigns remain operationally active.
vectors.asymmetric: Narrative persistence is a lagging indicator. By the time it is documented in Tier 1 sources, the campaign has already achieved multi-week reach.

vectors.name: Amplification Reach
vectors.headline: No new amplification-reach data this week; baseline posture unchanged
vectors.summary: No new bot-network disclosures, amplification-reach measurements, or coordinated-amplification findings were published this week.
vectors.detail: Amplification reach is measured by bot-network scale, coordinated-amplification patterns, and cross-platform coordination. No new data this week. Baseline posture: Russia and China operate high-volume amplification networks; Iran operates lower-volume but persistent networks; Gulf, US, and Israeli amplification reach is underdocumented.
vectors.asymmetric: Amplification reach is a structural power asymmetry. Actors with larger bot networks and cross-platform coordination capacity have disproportionate narrative control.

vectors.name: AI-enabled FIMI
vectors.headline: No new AI-enabled FIMI disclosures this week; baseline posture unchanged
vectors.summary: No new AI-generated content, synthetic media, or AI-assisted FIMI operations were disclosed this week.
vectors.detail: AI-enabled FIMI is measured by AI-generated text, synthetic media (deepfakes, voice clones), and AI-assisted content production at scale. No new disclosures this week. Baseline posture: Russia uses AI-assisted content production in multi-platform operations (Doppelganger, IRA-linked); China uses AI-generated text in Spamouflage operations; Iran, Gulf, US, and Israeli AI-FIMI capabilities are underdocumented.
vectors.asymmetric: AI content alone is not attribution evidence. MF2 applies. AI-enabled FIMI is a capability trajectory, not a current-state crisis.

vectors.name: Regulatory Enforcement Gap
vectors.headline: No new DSA enforcement actions this week; structural gap between DSA obligations and platform compliance persists
vectors.summary: EU DSA Article 40 FIMI provisions are in force, but no new enforcement actions, fines, or compliance reports were published this week.
vectors.detail: DSA Article 40 requires VLOPs to assess and mitigate FIMI risks. No new enforcement actions were published in the May 9-15 window. Structural gap: DSA obligations are binding, but enforcement is episodic and reactive. Platforms publish biannual or quarterly transparency reports; regulators publish enforcement actions ad hoc. This creates multi-week gaps where compliance status is unknown.
vectors.asymmetric: Regulatory enforcement gap is a structural feature of DSA implementation, not an incidental failure. Real-time compliance monitoring is impossible under current models.

module_9.title: Regulatory and Policy
module_9.digest_note: No new regulatory or policy developments this week.

no_change.status: In force since 2023; no new operational effects documented this week

module_9.friction_analysis: Regulatory friction is highest at the enforcement layer. DSA obligations are binding, but enforcement is episodic. Platforms publish transparency reports on their own schedules; regulators publish enforcement actions ad hoc. This creates multi-week gaps where compliance status is unknown. The structural gap between obligation and enforcement is the primary friction point.
module_9.dsa_fimi_status: DSA Article 40 FIMI provisions are in force. VLOPs are required to assess and mitigate FIMI risks in their biannual transparency reports. No new enforcement actions were published this week. Critical path: next VLOP transparency reports (estimated September 2026 for Meta, Google, TikTok) and next Commission enforcement action (timing unknown).

governance_gaps.gap: Real-time FIMI monitoring impossible under current platform disclosure models
governance_gaps.analysis: Meta publishes biannual reports; Google TAG publishes quarterly bulletins; Microsoft MSTIC publishes ad hoc. This creates multi-week gaps where no new Tier 1 attribution evidence is available. Real-time FIMI monitoring requires continuous disclosure, not episodic reporting.
governance_gaps.asymmetric: Episodic disclosure is a structural feature, not an incidental failure. It systematically favors actors who can operate between disclosure cycles.

governance_gaps.gap: Six-actor parity is methodologically required but evidentially impossible under current institutional models
governance_gaps.analysis: Russia, China, and Iran dominate Tier 1 disclosures. Gulf, US, and Israeli operations are structurally underdocumented. This is not an analytical failure but a structural feature of the FIMI monitoring ecosystem.
governance_gaps.asymmetric: Attribution gap systematically favors actors with adversarial relationships to Western platforms and institutions, creating structural bias in FIMI visibility.

module_12.title: AI-enabled FIMI and Technical Infrastructure
asymmetric_flags: AI content alone is not attribution evidence. MF2 applies to all AI-FIMI findings.

module_13.title: Information Environment

structural_trends.title: Platform disclosure cadence creates structural visibility gaps
structural_trends.trend: Meta publishes biannual reports; Google TAG publishes quarterly bulletins; Microsoft MSTIC publishes ad hoc. This creates multi-week gaps where no new Tier 1 attribution evidence is available. The current gap (9 weeks since Meta, 15 weeks since Google TAG) is within normal reporting cycles but leaves real-time FIMI monitoring dependent on Tier 2-3 sources with lower attribution confidence.

asymmetric_flags: Absence of disclosure does not equal absence of activity. Structural reporting gaps are a feature of the FIMI monitoring ecosystem, not an incidental failure.
